title: Residual Connections for Very Deep Convolutional Networks
abstract: We study how identity-mapping shortcut connections affect optimization difficulty in convolutional networks with over one hundred layers. By reformulating layers as learning residual functions relative to the layer inputs, we show that substantially deeper architectures can be trained without the degradation problem typically observed as depth increases. We report improved accuracy on standard image classification benchmarks and analyze how gradient flow is preserved through the added skip paths.
authors: L. Huang, M. Torres, S. Okafor

title: Self-Attention Mechanisms for Sequence Transduction Without Recurrence
abstract: We propose a sequence-to-sequence architecture built entirely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolution altogether. The model computes representations of its input and output using stacked self-attention layers, which allows for substantially more parallelization during training. On machine translation benchmarks the approach achieves competitive quality while requiring a fraction of the training time of comparable recurrent models.
authors: A. Vasilenko, R. Chen, T. Yamada

title: Bidirectional Pretraining Objectives for Contextual Language Representations
abstract: We introduce a pretraining procedure that jointly conditions on both left and right context in all layers, in contrast to prior left-to-right language modeling objectives. The resulting representations can be fine-tuned with a single additional output layer to achieve strong results across a broad range of language understanding tasks including question answering and natural language inference.
authors: K. Devlin, M. Chang, P. Lee

title: Graph Neural Networks for Relational Reasoning over Knowledge Bases
abstract: We present a message-passing framework for learning node and edge representations over multi-relational graphs. The architecture aggregates neighborhood information conditioned on relation type and demonstrates improved link-prediction accuracy on several knowledge-graph completion benchmarks compared to translation-based embedding methods.
authors: D. Fontaine, S. Iyer, W. Zhao

title: Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Input Smoothing
abstract: We analyze a certification technique in which Gaussian noise is added to network inputs at inference time, yielding provable robustness guarantees against small perturbations. We derive tight certified radii as a function of the noise level and empirically evaluate the trade-off between clean accuracy and certified robustness on image classifiers.
authors: J. Cohen, E. Rosenfeld, Z. Kolter

title: Few-Shot Text Generation via In-Context Demonstrations
abstract: We study the ability of large autoregressive language models to perform new tasks from only a handful of demonstrations provided in the prompt, without any gradient updates. Scaling model size substantially improves few-shot performance across translation, question answering, and cloze-style completion tasks, sometimes approaching fine-tuned baselines.
authors: T. Brown, N. Ryder, M. Subbiah

title: Contrastive Representation Learning for Unlabeled Visual Data
abstract: We present a simple framework for learning visual representations by maximizing agreement between differently augmented views of the same image via a contrastive loss in latent space. We systematically study the contribution of data augmentation composition, a learnable nonlinear projection head, and larger batch sizes to representation quality on downstream classification tasks.
authors: T. Chen, S. Kornblith, M. Norouzi

title: Differentiable Neural Architecture Search under Latency Constraints
abstract: We formulate architecture search as a continuous optimization problem over a weighted mixture of candidate operations, enabling gradient-based search rather than reinforcement learning or evolutionary methods. A latency-aware regularization term biases the search toward architectures that satisfy on-device inference budgets while maintaining competitive accuracy.
authors: H. Cai, L. Zhu, S. Han

title: Vision Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
abstract: We show that a pure transformer architecture applied directly to sequences of image patches, without convolutional inductive biases, can match or exceed convolutional network performance on image classification when pretrained on sufficiently large datasets. We study the data-scale regimes at which the transformer's lack of translation-equivariance ceases to be a disadvantage.
authors: A. Dosovitskiy, L. Beyer, A. Kolesnikov

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
abstract: We combine a pretrained sequence-to-sequence generator with a differentiable dense retriever over a large external document index, allowing the model to condition its output on retrieved passages rather than relying solely on parameters. This approach improves factual accuracy on open-domain question answering and reduces hallucinated content compared to purely parametric generation models.
authors: P. Lewis, E. Perez, A. Piktus

title: Federated Optimization with Heterogeneous Client Data
abstract: We examine convergence behavior of federated averaging algorithms when client data distributions are non-identically distributed across devices. We propose a variance-reduction correction term applied at the client level and show empirically that it narrows the accuracy gap relative to centralized training under moderate to severe data heterogeneity.
authors: S. Karimireddy, S. Kale, M. Mohri

title: Instruction Tuning Improves Zero-Shot Generalization of Language Models
abstract: We fine-tune a language model on a large collection of tasks phrased as natural language instructions and evaluate zero-shot performance on held-out task categories. Instruction tuning substantially improves generalization to unseen tasks compared to the base pretrained model, and gains increase with the number and diversity of instruction datasets used during fine-tuning.
authors: J. Wei, M. Bosma, V. Zhao

title: Diffusion Probabilistic Models for High-Fidelity Image Synthesis
abstract: We study a class of generative models that learn to reverse a gradual noising process applied to training images. By parameterizing the reverse process with a neural network trained to predict the added noise at each step, the model can synthesize high-fidelity samples and supports conditional generation via classifier guidance.
authors: J. Ho, A. Jain, P. Abbeel

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Multi-Step Reasoning in Language Models
abstract: We show that prompting large language models with a small number of exemplars that include intermediate reasoning steps significantly improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks compared to standard few-shot prompting, and that this benefit emerges only at sufficiently large model scale.
authors: J. Wei, X. Wang, D. Schuurmans

title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning via Low-Rank Adaptation
abstract: We propose freezing pretrained model weights and injecting trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into each transformer layer, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters required for downstream adaptation. The method matches full fine-tuning quality on several benchmarks while reducing GPU memory requirements and enabling fast task switching.
authors: E. Hu, Y. Shen, P. Wallis

title: Retrieval-Augmented Code Completion for Low-Resource Programming Languages
abstract: We investigate augmenting code-completion models with a retrieval component that surfaces similar code snippets from an indexed corpus at inference time. For low-resource languages with limited training data, retrieval augmentation improves completion accuracy substantially more than for high-resource languages, suggesting the retriever partially compensates for sparse pretraining coverage.
authors: R. Parvez, W. Ahmad, S. Chakraborty

title: Scaling Laws for Mixture-of-Experts Language Models
abstract: We empirically characterize how loss scales with total parameter count, number of active experts, and training compute for sparsely activated mixture-of-experts transformers. We find that at matched training compute, MoE architectures achieve lower loss than dense models, though the routing overhead introduces additional considerations for hardware utilization.
authors: N. Du, Y. Huang, A. Dai

title: Membership Inference Attacks against Fine-Tuned Language Models
abstract: We evaluate the extent to which an adversary with query access to a fine-tuned language model can determine whether a particular example was part of the fine-tuning set. We find that models fine-tuned on small, domain-specific datasets are considerably more vulnerable than base pretrained models, and propose a differential-privacy-based mitigation with a measured utility cost.
authors: F. Mireshghallah, K. Goyal, A. Backurs

title: Long-Context Transformers via Linear Attention Approximations
abstract: We study kernel-based approximations to softmax attention that reduce the quadratic time and memory complexity of self-attention to linear in sequence length. We benchmark several approximation schemes on long-document summarization and genomic sequence modeling, characterizing the accuracy-efficiency trade-off relative to exact attention.
authors: Y. Tay, M. Dehghani, D. Bahri

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Coordinated Robotic Manipulation
abstract: We train a team of simulated robotic arms to jointly manipulate objects that no single arm can move alone, using a centralized critic with decentralized execution. We introduce a curriculum that gradually increases object mass and find that it substantially improves coordination compared to training directly on the target task difficulty.
authors: M. Yu, W. Yang, T. Rashid

title: Byzantine-Resilient Distributed Training over Unreliable Networks
abstract: We propose a gradient-aggregation rule that bounds the influence of adversarial or faulty workers in distributed synchronous SGD without requiring a trusted coordinator. We prove convergence guarantees under a bounded fraction of Byzantine participants and evaluate wall-clock overhead relative to standard all-reduce training on commodity clusters.
authors: P. Blanchard, E. El Mhamdi, R. Guerraoui

title: Automated Theorem Proving with Language-Model-Guided Search
abstract: We combine a learned policy for proposing proof tactics with a classical tree-search procedure over a formal proof assistant's tactic state. The learned policy is trained on a corpus of human-written proofs and substantially reduces the number of search nodes expanded relative to uniform-cost tactic search on a benchmark of undergraduate-level mathematics problems.
authors: S. Polu, I. Sutskever

title: Energy-Based Calibration of Neural Network Confidence Estimates
abstract: We show that standard softmax confidence scores are poorly calibrated under distribution shift and propose an energy-based post-hoc recalibration procedure that requires no retraining of the base classifier. The method improves expected calibration error across several out-of-distribution benchmarks while leaving clean-data accuracy unchanged.
authors: W. Liu, X. Wang, J. Owens

title: Sparse Mixture Routing for Efficient On-Device Speech Recognition
abstract: We adapt sparse expert-routing techniques to streaming automatic speech recognition on resource-constrained devices, activating only a subset of acoustic-model parameters per audio frame. Compared to a dense baseline of equal accuracy, the routed model reduces per-frame compute substantially while meeting real-time latency constraints on mobile hardware.
authors: A. Gulati, J. Qin, C.-C. Chiu

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Atari Game Play from Raw Pixels
abstract: We present an agent that learns control policies directly from high-dimensional pixel input using a convolutional network trained with a variant of Q-learning. Experience replay and a separate target network stabilize training, and the resulting agent surpasses human-level performance on the majority of games in a benchmark suite without game-specific feature engineering.
authors: V. Mnih, K. Kavukcuoglu, D. Silver

title: Generative Adversarial Networks for Realistic Image Synthesis
abstract: We train two networks in a minimax game: a generator that produces synthetic samples and a discriminator that distinguishes generated samples from real data. As training proceeds the generator's outputs become increasingly difficult for the discriminator to distinguish from genuine samples, and we analyze conditions under which this adversarial process converges.
authors: I. Goodfellow, J. Pouget-Abadie, M. Mirza

title: Capsule Networks with Dynamic Routing Between Layers
abstract: We propose replacing scalar-output feature detectors with vector-output capsules whose length encodes the probability that an entity is present and whose orientation encodes instantiation parameters. An iterative routing-by-agreement mechanism determines how lower-level capsules contribute to higher-level ones, improving robustness to affine transformations relative to standard convolutional pooling.
authors: S. Sabour, N. Frosst, G. Hinton

title: Program Synthesis from Input-Output Examples via Neural-Guided Search
abstract: We combine a neural network that predicts promising next program tokens with a symbolic search procedure over a domain-specific language, guiding the search toward programs consistent with a small set of provided input-output examples. The neural guidance reduces the number of candidate programs explored by an order of magnitude relative to unguided enumerative search.
authors: K. Ellis, D. Ritchie, A. Solar-Lezama

title: Continual Learning without Catastrophic Forgetting via Synaptic Consolidation
abstract: We introduce a regularization term that penalizes changes to network parameters estimated to be important for previously learned tasks, computed from an online approximation of parameter importance accumulated during training. The approach mitigates catastrophic forgetting in sequential multi-task settings without storing examples from earlier tasks.
authors: J. Kirkpatrick, R. Pascanu, N. Rabinowitz

title: Curriculum Learning for Sample-Efficient Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We study automatic curriculum generation that orders training tasks by estimated difficulty, using an auxiliary predictor of episode success probability to select the next task for a sparse-reward robotic manipulation agent. The curriculum substantially reduces the number of environment interactions required to reach a target success rate compared to training on the full task distribution from the start.
authors: R. Portelas, C. Colas, K. Hofmann
